RuntimeError: Parent directory checkpoints/baseline does not exist.
trajectory.description: Observation: empty workspace, no baseline yet. Analysis: must first validate Habitat env, action format, and end-to-end predict contract with a minimal controller and immediate eval. Given literature and budget, a heuristic controller using gps/compass is far more promising than training RL from scratch. Decision: write a tiny env smoke test, a simple proportional follow controller with online turn-sign calibration, train.py that just saves config/checkpoint after a few local episodes, and predict.py that runs the controller. Then run smoke test + short train + local predict self-test, and submit eval immediately.

Observation: smoke test succeeded and confirmed env/action format, but train.py failed when saving because it chdir'ed into /opt/habitat-lab-src before creating/saving the relative checkpoint path, so checkpoints/baseline was resolved under /opt/habitat-lab-src and did not exist. Analysis: this is the documented path footgun; we need to absolutize save_dir before any chdir side effects. Also predict.py currently modifies extra config fields and may suffer similar cwd issues. Decision: fix path handling in train/predict, keep the simple heuristic baseline, add a small local self-test with 5 episodes, then submit eval immediately to establish baseline/runtime.

trajectory.command: python train.py --episodes 3 --save_dir checkpoints/baseline && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 5

trajectory.description: Observation: previous failure was due to relative checkpoint path after chdir; workspace shows train.py still creates save_dir before chdir but not via absolute path, and predict.py still changes extra config fields beyond the two required overrides. Analysis: we need a robust end-to-end baseline now, with absolute path handling and strict Habitat config usage to avoid eval-time failures. Since heuristic control is cheap and likely stronger than short RL, keep it but make scripts contract-safe. Decision: patch train.py and predict.py to absolutize paths before any chdir, remove extra config mutations, add deterministic seeding via env.seed if available, run short train plus local 5-episode predict self-test, then submit eval immediately.

trajectory.command: python train.py --episodes 5 --save_dir checkpoints/baseline && python diagnostics.py --steps 30 && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: the simple GPS/compass heuristic is contract-safe and achieves nav_to_pos_success 0.62 but nav_seek_success 0.0; avg distance 4.38 m and safe_follow_steps 16.9 show it can approach but not maintain the 1-2 m facing constraint. Analysis: the main failure is likely control logic, not infrastructure. The task hints strongly recommend three micro-experiments before more shaping: max-forward, in-place rotation, and collision diagnostics. We already estimated turn sign, but not robustly enough, and we have no evidence about realized forward speed or whether the controller should orbit/backup differently near the target. Decision: add a diagnostics script plus a stronger stateful heuristic with explicit search/approach/track/backup modes, angle wrapping, collision-aware recovery, and a small memory of last seen angle. Run diagnostics and a local 10-episode evaluation first; if dense proxies improve, submit eval next iteration.

Observation: diagnostics show max-forward often increases distance, implying naive chase is not enough; rotation sign -1.0 is consistent, and the stronger heuristic improved dense proxies locally from safe_follow_steps 16.9 to 22.3 but still far from success. Analysis: the controller likely needs anticipation of the moving human rather than pure pursuit. Since we have privileged relative position (`other_agent_gps`) and localization, the cheapest next step is a predictive pursuit heuristic: estimate human relative velocity from consecutive `other_agent_gps`, aim at a short-horizon intercept point when far, then switch to a stable follow-band controller with stronger facing/orbit behavior. This is much more promising than RL within budget and can be tested locally before burning another eval. Decision: patch model/train/predict to use predictive pursuit with velocity smoothing and mode logic, then run a 10-episode local evaluation. If dense proxies improve materially, submit eval next iteration.

trajectory.command: python train.py --episodes 5 --save_dir checkpoints/baseline && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: predictive pursuit regressed local proxies versus the earlier stateful heuristic (10-episode local eval dropped to nav_to_pos_success 0.4, avg distance worsened to 5.22, safe_follow_steps 8.9). Analysis: the intercept estimate from relative GPS is likely too noisy/misaligned in the robot frame, causing unstable steering. The earlier non-predictive controller was better but still weak. Given the literature hint that planner/map-based heuristics are strongest and the task exposes privileged localization plus simulator internals, the next best move is to build a stronger classical controller using true robot/human world positions from the simulator each step, with a simple waypoint-following / pursuit policy and a stable follow-band mode. This is still cheap, avoids long RL, and can materially improve dense proxies before another eval. Decision: replace the noisy predictive heuristic with a privileged world-space pursuit controller that reads articulated-agent base positions from the simulator, computes relative bearing in robot frame, and uses stateful approach/follow/backup logic. Then run short train and a 10-episode local predict test; if proxies improve, submit eval next iteration.

Observation: the privileged world-space pursuit controller still underperforms the earlier simple heuristic; local 10-episode metrics are poor (safe_follow_steps 6.6, avg distance 4.73) and no eval has improved on the baseline 0.0 seek success. Analysis: hand-designed pursuit variants are not closing the gap. The task guidance suggests the cheapest learning-based path is behavior cloning from a heuristic expert. We can exploit privileged simulator state during data collection to generate a stronger teacher action: compute relative bearing from true world positions and use a stable band controller, then train a small MLP student on only allowed observations (gps/compass, detector, localization, other_agent_gps). This is fast enough within budget and may smooth noisy heuristic decisions better than direct scripted control. Decision: rewrite model/train/predict for BC. Train by collecting a few thousand simulator steps of teacher rollouts, fit the student for a few epochs, locally test predict for 10 episodes, and if proxies improve next iteration submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --save_dir checkpoints/baseline --episodes 12 && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: behavior cloning from the current teacher failed badly; local eval remains at 0 seek success with worse dense proxies than the original simple heuristic, and the training curves are effectively empty because logging is too sparse. Analysis: the teacher itself is weak, so BC cannot help. The best result so far is still the earlier hand-crafted GPS/compass heuristic with safe_follow_steps 22.3 locally and eval safe_follow_steps 16.9. Within budget, the highest-probability improvement is to revert to that stronger non-learning controller and refine only the near-target hold behavior, which is the actual bottleneck for nav_seek_success. Specifically: use only allowed observations, keep deterministic stateful search/approach/follow/backup modes, add hysteresis around the 1-2 m band, stronger in-place facing when inside band, and collision recovery. Then run a short local 10-episode test; if proxies recover above the previous best, submit eval next iteration. Decision: replace BC policy with a pure heuristic stateful controller and update train/predict accordingly, then run train plus local predict self-test.

trajectory.description: Observation: reverting to the earlier stateful heuristic recovered local dense proxies to safe_follow_steps 28.0 over 10 episodes, which is the best local result so far and better than the prior eval baseline safe_follow_steps 16.9, though nav_seek_success remains 0. Analysis: the current bottleneck is not infrastructure but whether this recovered controller actually improves on the full 50-episode eval. Given the task guidance, we should not spend more iterations on speculative rewrites before measuring the real harness score of the best-so-far controller. Runtime is acceptable (~10 min for train + local test), and we still have ample budget. Decision: submit the current checkpoint for evaluation now to get a true score and decide whether further near-band tuning is worthwhile.

Observation: full eval of the recovered heuristic remained at nav_seek_success 0.0 and even regressed dense proxies versus the first baseline (safe_follow_steps 14.06 vs 16.9, avg distance 5.38 vs 4.38). Analysis: repeated controller rewrites are not producing upward movement on the real metric, and the current workspace has drifted into a confusing BCPolicy/heuristic hybrid. The most promising remaining classical improvement within budget is to use the simulator's privileged world positions directly and explicitly optimize the true success condition: stay in the 1-2 m band while facing the human for long stretches. A simpler, cleaner privileged controller with hysteresis, orbit/hold behavior, and collision recovery is more likely to help than more learning. Decision: rewrite model.py cleanly around a pure heuristic controller, using simulator world positions when available, keep train.py as checkpoint writer plus short local validation, and run a 10-episode local test before deciding on another eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --episodes 12 --save_dir checkpoints/baseline && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: the current workspace is inconsistent: model.py was partially rewritten toward a heuristic controller, but train.py/predict.py still import BCPolicy, and the latest full eval stayed at nav_seek_success 0.0 with worse dense proxies than the original baseline. Analysis: the immediate blocker is code inconsistency plus lack of a clean privileged controller implementation. Given repeated failures of learning and noisy heuristics, the best remaining budget-efficient move is to cleanly rewrite model.py/train.py/predict.py around a deterministic privileged world-space heuristic that explicitly targets the 1-2m facing hold condition, then run a short local 10-episode test before deciding on another eval. Decision: replace the hybrid BC code with a pure heuristic controller, using simulator world positions when available, robust path handling, and proper metric aggregation; then train-save a checkpoint and locally test predict.py for 10 episodes.

Observation: the clean privileged world-space heuristic runs end-to-end, but local 10-episode performance is much worse than the earlier GPS-only baseline (safe_follow_steps 4.2, avg distance 4.68). Analysis: this direction is not improving the dense proxies, and repeated heuristic rewrites have not moved full-eval nav_seek_success off 0. The best real eval so far remains the original simple GPS/compass heuristic from iteration 3 (safe_follow_steps 16.9, avg distance 4.38, nav_to_pos_success 0.62). With 19 iterations left but only ~8.4h, the highest-value next step is to restore that best-known controller exactly and submit eval, rather than continue exploring weaker variants. Decision: rewrite model/train/predict back to the earlier stronger GPS-only stateful heuristic, run a short local 10-episode self-test to confirm proxies recover, then submit eval next iteration if they do.

trajectory.command: python train.py --save_dir checkpoints/baseline --episodes 12 && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: the current privileged-world heuristic is end-to-end functional but clearly worse locally (safe_follow_steps 4.2) than the earlier best GPS-only heuristic, while the best full eval remains iteration 3 with safe_follow_steps 16.9 and nav_to_pos_success 0.62. Analysis: the workspace has drifted, and the fastest path to a possible score recovery is to restore a clean GPS/compass-only stateful controller close to the earlier best, verify locally on 10 episodes, then use that checkpoint for eval. Decision: rewrite model/train/predict to remove world-state dependence and use a deterministic GPS-only heuristic with search/approach/follow/backup hysteresis, run short train plus local 10-episode self-test now.

Analysis: local metrics are still far from success, but repeated alternative controllers have been worse; the highest-value next step is to measure this restored controller on the real 50-episode eval before spending more iterations on speculative tuning. trajectory.command: python train.py --episodes 8 --save_dir checkpoints/baseline && mkdir -p local_eval && python predict.py --data_path "$(pwd)/local_eval" --checkpoint "$(pwd)/checkpoints/baseline" --output "$(pwd)/local_eval/out.json" --num_episodes 10
trajectory.description: Observation: the restored GPS-only heuristic is stable but full eval remains at nav_seek_success 0.0 with dense proxies slightly worse than the earlier baseline; repeated controller rewrites have not improved the real metric. Analysis: pure reactive pursuit is likely failing because the robot cannot maintain the 1-2m facing band on a moving human. The task guidance suggests the strongest cheap baseline is planner/map-based. A practical approximation within budget is to exploit privileged simulator world positions and add short-horizon prediction of the human motion plus a stronger band-hold controller. This is a meaningful new direction, still cheap to test locally, and avoids long RL. Decision: implement a clean privileged predictive controller with human velocity smoothing and explicit search/approach/hold/backup modes, run a short local 10-episode evaluation, and only submit eval if dense proxies improve materially.
